concepts.lesson: A circle is the set of all points in a plane that are at a fixed distance from a fixed point called the centre. This fixed distance is known as the radius. Every point on the boundary of the circle is equidistant from the centre, which gives the circle its perfectly symmetrical shape. Circles are widely used in geometry and appear frequently in real-life objects and designs.
concepts.real_example: The shape of a wheel or a coin.
key_points: All boundary points are equidistant from centre

concepts.lesson: The radius of a circle is the distance from the centre to any point on the circle, while the diameter is the distance across the circle passing through the centre. The diameter is always twice the radius. Understanding the relationship between radius and diameter is essential for applying formulas related to circles.
concepts.real_example: Measuring the radius of a circular table to find its size.

concepts.lesson: A chord is a line segment joining any two points on the circle, while an arc is a portion of the circle’s circumference between two points. The diameter is the longest possible chord. Chords lie inside the circle, whereas arcs form curved boundaries. These concepts help in understanding circle geometry and related theorems.
concepts.real_example: The straight edge and curved edge of a pizza slice.

concepts.lesson: A tangent to a circle is a straight line that touches the circle at exactly one point called the point of contact. Unlike a secant, a tangent does not cut through the circle. Tangents play an important role in circle theorems and construction-based problems.
concepts.real_example: A road touching a circular park boundary at one point.
key_points: Point is called point of contact

concepts.lesson: Important properties of tangents include that the radius drawn to the point of contact is perpendicular to the tangent, and that tangents drawn from an external point to a circle are equal in length. These properties are frequently used in proofs and problem-solving involving circles.
concepts.real_example: Two equal support ropes touching a circular pole from the same point.
key_points: Radius ⟂ tangent at point of contact
Two tangents from an external point are equal
